title: Elegy To The Memory Of An Unfortunate Lady
content:
What beckï¿½ning ghost, along the moon-light shade
Invites my steps, and points to yonder glade?
ï¿½Tis she!ï¿½but why that bleeding bosom gorï¿½d,
Why dimly gleams the visionary sword?
Oh ever beauteous, ever friendly! tell,
Is it, in heavï¿½n, a crime to love too well?
To bear too tender, or too firm a heart,
To act a loverï¿½s or a Romanï¿½s part?
Is there no bright reversion in the sky,
For those who greatly think, or bravely die?

Why bade ye else, ye powï¿½rs! her soul aspire
Above the vulgar flight of low desire?
Ambition first sprung from your blest abodes;
The glorious fault of angels and of gods;
Thence to their images on earth it flows,
And in the breasts of kings and heroes glows.
Most souls, ï¿½tis true, but peep out once an age,
Dull sullen prisï¿½ners in the bodyï¿½s cage:
Dim lights of life, that burn a length of years
Useless, unseen, as lamps in sepulchres;
Like eastern kings a lazy state they keep,
And close confinï¿½d to their own palace, sleep.

From these perhaps (ere nature bade her die)
Fate snatchï¿½d her early to the pitying sky.
As into air the purer spirits flow,
And sepï¿½rate from their kindred dregs below;
So flew the soul to its congenial place,
Nor left one virtue to redeem her race.

But thou, false guardian of a charge too good,
Thou, mean deserter of thy brotherï¿½s blood!
See on these ruby lips the trembling breath,
These cheeks now fading at the blast of death:
Cold is that breast which warmï¿½d the world before,
And those love-darting eyes must roll no more.
Thus, if eternal justice rules the ball,
Thus shall your wives, and thus your children fall;
On all the line a sudden vengeance waits,
And frequent hearses shall besiege your gates.
There passengers shall stand, and pointing say,
(While the long funï¿½rals blacken all the way)
ï¿½Lo these were they, whose souls the furies steelï¿½d,
And cursï¿½d with hearts unknowing how to yield.
Thus unlamented pass the proud away,
The gaze of fools, and pageant of a day!
So perish all, whose breast neï¿½er learnï¿½d to glow
For othersï¿½ good, or melt at othersï¿½ woe.ï¿½

What can atone (oh ever-injurï¿½d shade!)
Thy fate unpitied, and thy rites unpaid?
No friendï¿½s complaint, no kind domestic tear
Pleasï¿½d thy pale ghost, or gracï¿½d thy mournful bier.
By foreign hands thy dying eyes were closï¿½d,
By foreign hands thy decent limbs composï¿½d,
By foreign hands thy humble grave adornï¿½d,
By strangers honourï¿½d, and by strangers mournï¿½d!
What though no friends in sable weeds appear,
Grieve for an hour, perhaps, then mourn a year,
And bear about the mockery of woe
To midnight dances, and the public show?
What though no weeping loves thy ashes grace,
Nor polishï¿½d marble emulate thy face?
What though no sacred earth allow thee room,
Nor hallowï¿½d dirge be mutterï¿½d oï¿½er thy tomb?
Yet shall thy grave with rising flowï¿½rs be drest,
And the green turf lie lightly on thy breast:
There shall the morn her earliest tears bestow,
There the first roses of the year shall blow;
While angels with their silver wings oï¿½ershade
The ground, now sacred by thy reliques made.

So peaceful rests, without a stone, a name,
What once had beauty, titles, wealth, and fame.
How lovï¿½d, how honourï¿½d once, avails thee not,
To whom related, or by whom begot;
A heap of dust alone remains of thee,
ï¿½Tis all thou art, and all the proud shall be!

Poets themselves must fall, like those they sung,
Deaf the praisï¿½d ear, and mute the tuneful tongue.
Evï¿½n he, whose soul now melts in mournful lays,
Shall shortly want the genï¿½rous tear he pays;
Then from his closing eyes thy form shall part,
And the last pang shall tear thee from his heart,
Lifeï¿½s idle business at one gasp be oï¿½er,
The Muse forgot, and thou belovï¿½d no more!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alexander Pope represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Alexander Pope's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
